questions.question: Why should you avoid returning @Entity objects directly from a Spring Boot REST controller?
options: JPA entities are not serializable to JSON
It exposes sensitive fields, causes lazy-loading exceptions, and couples the API contract to the DB schema
Spring MVC cannot serialize entity objects — it only handles plain Java classes
Entities are too large to send over HTTP efficiently

questions.question: What is a Java Record and why is it well-suited for DTOs?
options: A record is a database row representation that maps directly to a table
A record is an immutable class with auto-generated constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString — zero boilerplate for DTOs
A record is a Spring annotation that marks a class as a DTO
A record is a Java interface that enforces the DTO pattern

questions.question: Where should the logic to map a User entity to a UserResponse DTO live?
options: In the @RestController before returning the response
In the @Entity class itself, since it knows its own fields
In a static factory method on the DTO (UserResponse.from(user)) or a dedicated mapper class
In a @Component bean called MappingHelper with one giant switch statement

questions.question: MapStruct generates mapping code at what point?
options: At runtime using reflection when the first mapping is called
At compile time — it generates Java implementation classes during the build
During Spring context initialization using dynamic proxies
On-demand when the application receives its first HTTP request

questions.question: A service method receives a CreateUserRequest DTO. What should it return?
options: The User entity after saving — the controller can do the mapping
void — the service should not return anything
A UserResponse DTO — the service layer should map entity to DTO before returning
The raw Map<String, Object> from the HTTP request body
